config.title: Measuring Progress
config.citation: Mahler, Daniel Gerszon, and Maarten Lambrechts. 2026. “Measuring Progress” In Atlas of Sustainable Development Goals 2026, edited by A. F. Pirlea, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, D. Wadhwa, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO. https://doi.org/10.60616/f7gk-me57
config.inputs: Daniel Boller, Bob Rijkers, Nishant Yonzan,

content.text: Measuring progress is not easy
Tracking progress towards the SDGs is crucial for countries to identify areas of development to prioritize and to learn from the successes and failures of other countries.

India’s challenge regarding open defecation ([target: 6.2]) offers a good example[emphasis: . ]Data revealed that India lagged behind its peers and its own target for ending open defecation before 2014. To address this, the Government of India launched the Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM), focusing on toilet construction and changing behavior regarding sanitation practices. The mission constructed over 100 million toilets by 2019, and the proportion of the population practicing open defecation fell from 33% in 2014 to 20% in 2019—a 40% drop in the span of five short years. [reference: swachh][reference: unsdg]

Cases like this illustrate how understanding countries’ progress can spearhead policy change and document achievements. Though India’s achievements were impressive, how can we quantify how well it did over this time span? It is not as trivial as it may sound.

When measuring progress towards an SDG, a common first step is to track whether a country has met a specific SDG target. This overlooks that a country like India may have made significant progress towards a target, but still fall short of it.[reference: easterly2009][reference: clemens2007] In India, despite its massive headways, more than 6% still practice open defecation, which is above the [target: 6.2][emphasis:  ]target calling for an end to open defecation.[reference: unsdg]

One could instead compare progress across countries looking at the changes they have experienced. In India, we could compare the decline in the proportion of people practicing open defecation of 40%—a measure of the change in percent—with what happened in other countries. Alternatively, we could compare India’s decline of 13 percentage points (from 33% to 20%), with its peers.

Unfortunately these two simple and frequently used measures of progress often give different answers to which country has made most progress. To see this, consider the Cote d’Ivoire and Georgia’s recent progress in reducing extreme poverty ([target: 1.1]).

content.title: Which country made the most progress?
content.source: World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform, World Bank Macro and Poverty Outlook October 2025.

scenes.text: Between 2015 and 2025, both countries made impressive reductions in poverty.  Cote d’Ivoire reduced poverty from 27.4% to 15.9% and Georgia from 10.6% to 3.6%.

If we compare the decline in poverty rates in [emphasis: percentage points], Cote d’Ivoire has a larger decline. Over the nine year horizon, 11.5 percent of the population of Georgia escaped poverty vis-à-vis about 7 percent of the population in the Philippines.

Yet Georgia could not possibly have matched Cote d’Ivoire’s feat as only 10.6% of the population was in poverty in 2015. If we instead look at the decline in [emphasis: percent] (share of the poor population that escaped poverty), then Georgia made the most progress. There, 66% of the poor escaped poverty vis-a-vis 42% in Cote d’Ivoire.

content.text: The speed of development

It's unclear which country has made more progress because standard measures provide conflicting answers. This is often because these common measures inherently favor or disadvantage specific stages of development.[reference: klasen2012]  To accurately gauge a country's progress, we need to adjust our analysis to reflect that significant changes are more likely during certain phases of development than others.[reference: allwine2016][reference: ravallion2012] Let’s look at how this can be done for  [target: 1.1]:

content.title: Annualized changes in poverty rates as a function of baseline poverty rates
content.source: World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform, World Bank Macro and Poverty Outlook October 2025, Mahler et al. (2025a), and Mahler et al. (2025b).

scenes.text: First, we plot all changes in poverty rates observed across countries going back to 1950 as a function of the baseline poverty rate.
Next, we add the line for which at every baseline poverty rate, approximately half the changes are worse and half the changes are better.
Changes in poverty are typically smallest (in absolute terms) for very low and very high poverty rates and largest for poverty rates around 50%.
A country with a poverty rate of 50% typically decreases poverty by 0.71 percentage points the following year…
…while a country with a poverty rate of 5% typically decreases poverty by 0.19 percentage points the following year.

From this, we can trace out a typical trajectory from any starting point. Let's start with Cote d’Ivoire poverty rate in 2015, 27.4%. For this poverty rate, the typical change in poverty rate is -0.65.
So in 2016, if Cote d’Ivoire had followed the typical pattern, the poverty rate would have been 26.7%.
Iteratively following this method, we can trace the typical path a country with Cote d’Ivoire’s starting point would have followed over ten years.
Next, we compare that trend with Cote d’Ivoire’s actual trend from 2015 to 2025.
What Cote d’Ivoire did from 2015 to 2025 normally would take until 2035. In other words, Cote d’Ivoire did in 10 years what normally takes 20 years and it can be said to have moved at a speed of two.
Georgia did in 10 years what normally takes 30 years, so its speed is three. This suggests that Georgia made faster progress than Cote d’Ivoire.
We can also look at the path for a country that starts entrenched in extreme poverty,
trace out the full trajectory if the country always develops at the typical speed of progress. Ending poverty at this speed takes more than two centuries.
We can now compare how far countries moved on this trajectory from 2015 and 2025.
This is the poverty rate of Cote d'Ivoire in 2015.
In 10 years, Cote d'Ivoire made progress that typically takes 20 years
In El Salvador, by contrast, no progress was made and it moved at a speed of zero.

In Malawi progress has been reversed, and in 10 years it undid progress that normally takes 10 years to achieve. As such, it can be said to have moved at a speed of -1, each year undoing about one year of typical progress.
If we add all countries, those that made large positive headways on the path had high speeds of progress, and vice versa.

content.text: The future and setting targets beyond 2030

At the typical speed of development, going from very high poverty rates to a society free of extreme poverty often takes centuries. This does not only pertain to poverty, but applies to other domains of development as well. Even if countries move drastically faster than the typical speed of development, like Singapore and Korea since the 1950s, transitioning from the worst observed outcomes to some of the best easily takes 75 years.

The long road to development suggests that some SDG targets, such as complete access to services or complete elimination of deprivations, were plausible for some countries but virtually impossible for countries that are generations from reaching these levels. Globally ambitious targets are harder to achieve for poorer countries which need much faster speeds of progress to obtain the same level as countries starting from better positions. Yet success and failure has been closely associated with the outcome of these targets.[reference: waage2010]
So how should countries set realistic and aspirational targets? The speed scores explained above can be used to create targets at varying levels of ambition and with various comparator countries.

content.title: Possible poverty targets for Cote d'Ivoire
content.subtitle: Cote d’Ivoire’s poverty rate is 15.9% in 2025. The path presented above suggests that if a country starts with a poverty rate of 15.9%, at the typical speed of progress, poverty would be driven down to 6.9% in 2050. This is one candidate target value for the country by 2050. However, Cote d’Ivoire moved twice as fast as the typical speed in the past decade. If it continues this speed, it will not take 25 years to reach 6.9% but only 12 years, so by 2037. If it continues this speed until 2050, it would reach a poverty rate of 2.7% by 2050. Cote d’Ivoire could choose an even more ambitious target, such as moving 4 times the historical speed (or approximately twice as fast as it did over the past decade). This would reflect some of the fastest movers we have seen. Under such a scenario, the country should target a 0.5% poverty rate by 2050.

content.text: Such high speeds of development are not necessarily what countries should consider for their targets. Some countries may have less capacity to develop due to high debt levels, conflict, or other reasons, and could choose a target based on their own, slower historical pace of progress. Countries could also choose the speed of an aspirational set of countries—say, the best performers in their region—to define a target.
Challenges when measuring progress
The method presented here and most other measures of progress face limitations that are important to be aware of.

First, the method cannot be applied to all indicators. The speed method does not work for indicators where countries on expectation do not make progress. For such indicators, we have developed a complementary way of measuring progress that gives all countries a score from 0-100. This score reflects the share of historical experiences a country is outperforming.[reference: mahler2025a] For example, a shore of 80 means that a country is progressing faster than 80% of all cases we have observed historically.

Second, measuring progress is difficult when indicators change drastically from year to year, such as when measuring deaths from natural disasters. For such variables, tracking progress is challenging, as a country may have made fast progress on its emergency preparedness and adaptation to climate change, yet one year face a rare natural disaster which could suggest a lack of progress.

Third, the method requires indicators with a large amount of data in order to estimate the expected changes with sufficient accuracy. This means that indicators that lack data over a long time period or over a large set of countries will involve greater uncertainty.

Finally, the method is limited by the fact that we do not observe all countries at all stages of development. For example, to estimate how countries typically progress at high levels of development, we can only rely on information from countries that have achieved such standards. Conversely, we do not have data at low levels of development for most of the countries that are currently highly developed. This impacts the transition paths, and had such data been available, they may have looked differently.
